pages.title: Welcome to Classroom Communication & Data Safety
pages.subtitle: Learn how to communicate across school platforms safely while reducing data exposure and cybersecurity risk.

video.caption: Introduction to Classroom Communication & Data Safety

pages.title: Why communication is also a cybersecurity issue
pages.text:
Classroom communication is not just about being clear. It is also about protecting information.

Messages between teachers, parents, administrators, counselors, and staff may include sensitive student information such as grades, behavior, accommodations, attendance, health-related details, or personal situations.

When that information is sent to the wrong person, shared through the wrong tool, or exposed in a file or message, it becomes a cybersecurity risk because sensitive data is being accessed or disclosed in ways that should not happen.

  • Wrong recipient can expose private student details
  • Wrong platform can move data outside school protections
  • Too much detail can create unnecessary exposure
  • Weak sharing habits can lead to unauthorized access

Privacy rules such as FERPA exist to help protect student records, but many school data incidents begin with everyday communication mistakes rather than hacking.

Cybersecurity is not just about stopping hackers. It also includes how sensitive information is shared.

pages.title: Use approved school communication channels
pages.text:
Student information should be shared only through approved school systems.

Examples may include:

  • School email
  • Official parent communication platforms
  • Approved learning management systems
  • District-approved messaging tools

Avoid using personal email, informal group chats, social media, or unapproved apps for student-related communication unless school policy clearly allows it.

Approved systems are usually better because they are more likely to support school oversight, secure access, and proper record handling.

Where you communicate matters because the wrong platform can create unnecessary data exposure.

pages.title: Email and messaging mistakes can expose data
pages.text:
Email and messaging mistakes are one of the easiest ways to expose student information.

Common risks include:

  • Sending a message to the wrong parent or guardian
  • Using autocomplete and selecting the wrong recipient
  • Replying all when sensitive information should stay private
  • Including student details in an informal chat or text thread
  • Forwarding a message that contains more student information than necessary

Before sending, pause and confirm who will receive the message, whether the platform is appropriate, and whether every detail in the message is truly needed.

Always double-check the recipient before sending any student-related message.

pages.title: Files, links, and permissions can create hidden risk
pages.text:
Not every communication risk is in the message itself. Sometimes the risk is in the file, link, or permission setting attached to it.

Examples include:

  • Sharing a grade sheet with the wrong people
  • Leaving a document set to Anyone with the link can view
  • Attaching the wrong file to an email
  • Sharing a folder that contains more student information than intended
  • Allowing others to edit a document that should be view-only

Always confirm who can open, view, or edit a file before sharing it.

A correct message can still become a security problem if the wrong file or permission is attached.

pages.title: Casual communication can still be a cyber risk
pages.text:
School data exposure does not only happen in formal emails. It can also happen through casual communication and everyday habits.

Risk can come from:

  • Informal staff chats
  • Text messages
  • Personal notes shared too widely
  • Conversations in public areas
  • Posting classroom details online
  • Leaving messages or files visible on a screen during a meeting

Even when the goal is to help, sensitive student information should not be shared where others may see, hear, copy, or forward it.

Informal communication can still expose sensitive student information to the wrong people.

image.alt: Casual communication risks in school settings

pages.title: What to do if you make a communication mistake
pages.text:
If you think student information was sent, posted, or shared incorrectly:

  1. Do not ignore it
  2. Stop further sharing if possible
  3. Notify the appropriate administrator, privacy contact, or designated staff member
  4. Report the issue according to school policy

Quick reporting helps the school reduce further exposure, assess who was affected, and respond appropriately.

Report communication mistakes quickly so the school can reduce further exposure.
